---
title: "Ch 12 — Factorisation"
subtitle: "Run the multiplication backwards — turn a sum into a product"
lesson-id: "cbse8.factorisation"
tier: "cbse8"
ncert-class: 8
ncert-chapter: 12
ncert-chapter-title: "Factorisation"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse8.algebraic-expressions", "t1.divisibility-primes"]
unlocks: ["cbse9.polynomials", "cbse10.polynomials", "cbse10.quadratic-equations"]
applied-domains: ["research", "encryption"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you expand $(x + 3)(x + 5)$? If not, revisit
   **[Algebraic Expressions and Identities](../08-algebraic-expressions/index.qmd)**.
2. Do you remember the three identities $(a+b)^2$, $(a-b)^2$, and
   $(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2$?
3. Can you write $24$ as a product of primes? If not, revisit
   **[Divisibility and Primes](../../tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)**.
4. Do you know what the *greatest common factor (GCF)* of $12$ and
   $18$ is?

If three were a confident yes, read on.
:::

## Touch

Take $12$ identical buttons (or coins). Arrange them in rectangles —
*all* the rectangles you can make.

You will find: $1 \times 12$, $2 \times 6$, $3 \times 4$. (And the
mirror images.) Each rectangle is a "factoring" of $12$ into a product
of two whole numbers.

The widget below does the same for variable expressions.

```{ojs}
viewof expr = Inputs.select(
  ["6x + 9", "x^2 + 5x", "x^2 + 7x + 12", "x^2 - 16"],
  {label: "Expression"}
)
```

```{ojs}
{
  const e = expr;
  const map = {
    "6x + 9":         "= 3 &middot; (2x + 3)",
    "x^2 + 5x":       "= x &middot; (x + 5)",
    "x^2 + 7x + 12":  "= (x + 3) &middot; (x + 4)",
    "x^2 - 16":       "= (x - 4) &middot; (x + 4)",
  };
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.6;">
    <div><b>${e}</b> &nbsp; ${map[e]}</div>
    <div style="color:#666;margin-top:0.4rem;">
      Each expression is being shown as a product of simpler pieces.
      How would you find that product *yourself*?
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Each example *is* a product, written as a sum. The job of this chapter
is to run the rectangle-game in reverse: given the sum, recover the
product.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. In $6x + 9$, what number divides both terms? What about $x^2 + 5x$
   — is there a *variable* that divides both?
2. We discovered $(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2$ in the last chapter. So if
   you see $x^2 - 25$, what should you reach for?
3. The expression $x^2 + 7x + 12$ has constant $12$ and middle
   coefficient $7$. Can you guess the two numbers we'll need?
4. Could every algebraic sum be factorised into two simpler pieces? Or
   are some sums "prime"?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice (don't peek until you've thought)

Factorisation has four main techniques: pull out the **common
factor**, group, recognise an **identity** ($a^2 - b^2$, $a^2 + 2ab +
b^2$, $a^2 - 2ab + b^2$), and split the middle term. Some expressions
are irreducible — like prime numbers in algebra-land — and stay sums.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Common factor

Factorise $6x + 9$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
What is the GCF of $6$ and $9$?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
GCF $= 3$. So $6x + 9 = 3(2x + 3)$.

Check by expanding: $3 \cdot 2x + 3 \cdot 3 = 6x + 9$. ✓
</details>

### Problem 2 — Variable common factor

Factorise $x^2 + 5x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
What divides both $x^2$ and $5x$?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Both contain $x$. So $x^2 + 5x = x(x + 5)$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Group and factor

Factorise $ax + ay + bx + by$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The first two terms share an $a$; the last two share a $b$. Group
them.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$a(x + y) + b(x + y)$ — both pieces share $(x+y)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$ax + ay + bx + by = a(x + y) + b(x + y) = (x + y)(a + b)$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Difference of squares

Factorise $x^2 - 49$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$49 = 7^2$. Identity: $a^2 - b^2 = (a+b)(a-b)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 - 49 = (x + 7)(x - 7)$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Perfect square

Factorise $x^2 + 10x + 25$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$25 = 5^2$ and $10 = 2 \cdot 5$. Identity: $(a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 + 10x + 25 = (x + 5)^2$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Split the middle term

Factorise $x^2 + 7x + 12$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You need two numbers whose *sum* is $7$ and *product* is $12$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Factors of $12$: $1 \cdot 12, 2 \cdot 6, 3 \cdot 4$. Of these, $3 + 4 =
7$. So split: $7x = 3x + 4x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 + 7x + 12 = x^2 + 3x + 4x + 12 = x(x + 3) + 4(x + 3) = (x + 3)(x +
4)$.
</details>

### Problem 7 — Polynomial division

Divide $6x^3 - 8x^2 + 4x$ by $2x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pull the common factor $2x$ from each term.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$6x^3 - 8x^2 + 4x = 2x(3x^2 - 4x + 2)$. Dividing by $2x$: **$3x^2 - 4x +
2$**.

Polynomial division is just factorisation followed by cancellation when
the divisor is a factor.
</details>

You have just used four techniques: common factor, grouping, identity,
and middle-term split. The chapter is the choreography of switching
between them.

::: {.fun-fact}
The Greek mathematician **Diophantus of Alexandria** (c. 200 – c. 284
CE), in his *Arithmetica*, was the first to write equations in something
resembling modern symbolic form — and the first to *factor* expressions
to solve them. A millennium and a half later, his book sat on Pierre de
Fermat's desk; in its margin, next to a problem on writing a square as a
sum of two squares, Fermat scribbled the conjecture that became *Fermat's
Last Theorem* — finally proved by Andrew Wiles in 1995. Every chain of
factoring you do in this chapter is in Diophantus' direct line.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
Factoring is *running multiplication backwards*. The identities you
proved in Chapter 8 — $(a+b)^2$, $(a-b)^2$,
[difference of squares](/glossary.qmd#difference-of-squares) — are the
same equalities, read right-to-left. The chapter's only new skill is
**pattern-recognition**: looking at a sum and seeing the product hiding
inside.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

To **factorise** an expression is to write it as a *product* of simpler
expressions whose product equals the original.

**The four techniques:**

1. **Common factor.** Pull out the largest expression dividing every
   term.
2. **Grouping.** Pair terms so each pair has a common factor; pull
   out the shared binomial.
3. **Identity.** Recognise $a^2 - b^2$, $(a \pm b)^2$, etc., and
   apply.
4. **Splitting the middle term.** For $x^2 + bx + c$, find numbers
   $p, q$ with $p + q = b$ and $pq = c$, then split $bx = px + qx$ and
   group.

**Polynomial division** is just factorisation: if $g(x)$ divides
$f(x)$, then $f(x) = g(x) \cdot q(x)$, and the quotient is what is
left after extracting the factor.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Pull the common, group the four, spot the identity-shape;*
> *Or split the middle into two whose sum is right, whose product is too.*
> *Then the sum has become a product —*
> *And the product reveals what the sum could not.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Factorise $4x^2 - 25y^2$.

Competent solver's path:

1. Spot the shape: difference of two squares.
2. $4x^2 = (2x)^2$ and $25y^2 = (5y)^2$.
3. Apply $a^2 - b^2 = (a+b)(a-b)$ with $a = 2x$, $b = 5y$.

**Answer: $4x^2 - 25y^2 = (2x + 5y)(2x - 5y)$.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Factorise $12x + 8$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4(3x + 2)$.
</details>

**W2.** Factorise $a^2 - 81$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(a + 9)(a - 9)$.
</details>

**W3.** Factorise $y^2 + 6y + 9$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(y + 3)^2$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Factorise $a^2 - 14a + 49$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$49 = 7^2$ and $14 = 2 \cdot 7$. Perfect square of a difference.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(a - 7)^2$.
</details>

**P2.** Factorise $x^2 + 9x + 18$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two numbers with sum $9$ and product $18$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 + 6 = 9$, $3 \cdot 6 = 18$. Split: $9x = 3x + 6x$.
$x^2 + 3x + 6x + 18 = x(x + 3) + 6(x + 3) = (x + 3)(x + 6)$.
</details>

**P3.** Factorise $6x^2 + 17x + 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For $ax^2 + bx + c$, find two numbers whose product is $ac$ and whose
sum is $b$. Here $ac = 30$, $b = 17$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$15 \cdot 2 = 30$, $15 + 2 = 17$. Split $17x = 15x + 2x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$6x^2 + 17x + 5 = 6x^2 + 15x + 2x + 5 = 3x(2x + 5) + 1(2x + 5) =
(2x + 5)(3x + 1)$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Factorise $a^4 - b^4$ completely.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Difference of squares applied twice.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$a^4 - b^4 = (a^2)^2 - (b^2)^2$. Now factor again.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a^4 - b^4 = (a^2 + b^2)(a^2 - b^2) = (a^2 + b^2)(a + b)(a - b)$.

(Note: $a^2 + b^2$ is *not* further factorisable using real-number
techniques.)
</details>

**S2.** Factorise $x^3 + 2x^2 - x - 2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Group the four terms in pairs.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Group: $(x^3 + 2x^2) - (x + 2) = x^2(x + 2) - 1(x + 2)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^3 + 2x^2 - x - 2 = x^2(x+2) - 1(x+2) = (x+2)(x^2 - 1) = (x+2)(x+1)(x-1)$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** $x^2 - 9$ factorises as
(a) $(x-3)(x+3)$ (b) $(x-9)(x+9)$ (c) $(x-3)^2$ (d) $(x+3)^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Difference of squares. **Answer: (a).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Factorise $25x^2 - 70xy + 49y^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$25x^2 = (5x)^2$ and $49y^2 = (7y)^2$ and $70xy = 2 \cdot 5x \cdot 7y$.
$(a-b)^2$ shape.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(5x - 7y)^2$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Factorise $x^2 + x - 132$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two numbers with product $-132$ and sum $1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Try $12$ and $-11$: product $-132$, sum $1$. ✓
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 + x - 132 = x^2 + 12x - 11x - 132 = x(x + 12) - 11(x + 12) =
(x + 12)(x - 11)$.

Check: $(x+12)(x-11) = x^2 - 11x + 12x - 132 = x^2 + x - 132$. ✓
</details>

**4-mark (long answer).** (i) Factorise $a^2 - 2ab + b^2 - c^2$.
(ii) Hence find the value when $a = 7, b = 4, c = 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The first three terms form a perfect square. Then a difference of
squares appears.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

**(i)** $a^2 - 2ab + b^2 = (a-b)^2$. So
$a^2 - 2ab + b^2 - c^2 = (a-b)^2 - c^2 = (a - b + c)(a - b - c)$.

**(ii)** $a - b = 3$. So $(a-b+c) = 8$ and $(a-b-c) = -2$.
Product: $8 \times (-2) = -16$.

(Verify by direct substitution: $49 - 56 + 16 - 25 = -16$. ✓)
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Problem 12.6 ("factorise $a^2 -
b^2 + 2bc - c^2$") is a near-twin of the 4-mark item — both use
*group three terms as a square, then difference-of-squares*.
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2019 Set 1, Q11 ("factorise $x^2 + 6x
+ 8$") matches *Working P2* exactly.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$a^2 + b^2 = (a + b)(a - b)$."** No — that's $a^2 - b^2$. The
   *sum* of two squares does not factor over the real numbers (in this
   chapter's toolkit).
2. **"Splitting the middle term works for any trinomial."** Only for
   trinomials of the form $ax^2 + bx + c$ where the discriminant
   $b^2 - 4ac$ is a perfect square. Otherwise the roots are irrational
   and you need the formula from Class 10.
3. **"$x^2 + 1$ can be factorised."** Not over the real numbers. You
   meet its complex factorisation $(x + i)(x - i)$ later, in Tier 4.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

1. To [factor](/glossary.qmd#factoring) is to write a sum as a product —
   the inverse of "expand". The product reveals the *structure* (roots,
   common pieces) that the sum hid.
2. Four techniques cover almost everything in Class 8: **common
   factor**, **grouping**, **identity-spot**, and **middle-term split**.
   Try them in that order.
3. Some sums are irreducible — like primes among the integers. $x^2 +
   1$, $x^2 + x + 1$, and $a^2 + b^2$ stay sums under real-number
   factoring; meeting them is *normal*, not failure.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you state the four techniques in order, and pick the right
   one for a problem you've never seen?
2. Did you solve at least 6 of the 7 *Discover* problems and 5 of the
   7 *Practice* problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Given a trinomial, can you split the middle term in under thirty
   seconds?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Introduction to Graphs](../13-introduction-to-graphs/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo *Working P3* and the 4-mark board item, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** rebuild from
  **[Algebraic Expressions and Identities](../08-algebraic-expressions/index.qmd)** —
  factorisation runs the same identities backwards, so the forward
  direction must be airtight.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Algebraic Expressions and Identities](../08-algebraic-expressions/index.qmd)** — the three standard identities, ready to be reversed.
- **[Divisibility and Primes](../../tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)** — finding the GCF of integer coefficients.

### What this unlocks

- **[Class 9 — Polynomials](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/02-polynomials/index.qmd)** — adds Remainder and Factor theorems for cubics.
- **[Class 10 — Polynomials](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/02-polynomials/index.qmd)** — relations between roots and coefficients.
- **[Class 10 — Quadratic Equations](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/04-quadratic-equations/index.qmd)** — solving by factorisation, the formula, completing the square.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 8, Chapter 12 — Factorisation** (NCERT 2023 rationalised
  syllabus). Covers Sections 12.1–12.4: common factors, grouping,
  identity-based factorisation, and division of polynomials by
  monomials.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different pedagogical angle):
  [`t2.factoring-polynomials`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/12-factoring-polynomials/index.qmd)
  treats factoring at the synthesis level; this chapter focuses on
  computational technique and the four-pattern recognition drill.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**, which remains the canonical
  source for the board paper.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Factoring polynomials is the discrete cousin of factoring
integers. The same algorithms — Gauss's method, Berlekamp's
algorithm — show up in symbolic computation systems like Mathematica
and SymPy.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** RSA security depends on a *very specific*
factorisation problem being hard: factoring a $2048$-bit integer that
is the product of two large primes. The fact that *integer*
factorisation is hard while *polynomial* factorisation (today's lesson)
is easy is the unsung asymmetry of modern cryptography.
:::
